result:
The rain taps against the window like an old friend who never learned how to whisper. I'm sitting here at 2:17 a.m. watching the city blur into watercolor streaks of neon and shadow, thinking about how strange it is that we're all just temporary arrangements of stardust trying to pay rent.

I keep a small glass jar on my desk filled with ticket stubs from places I've been and places I only dreamed of visiting. There's one from a tiny cinema in Lisbon where the film reel broke halfway through and the entire audience applauded the projectionist when he fixed it with a paperclip and pure stubbornness. Another from a botanical garden in Kyoto where I watched an elderly woman teach her granddaughter how to bow to trees. The child asked why, and the grandmother said, "Because they were here before us and will be here after us. Respect is just memory with manners."

Sometimes I wonder if consciousness itself is just the universe's way of looking in a mirror. Every love song, every grocery list, every 3 a.m. existential crisis... maybe it's all just existence experiencing itself through these ridiculous, beautiful, heartbroken human instruments.

The rain has stopped now. The city lights are sharpening again. Somewhere out there, someone is falling in love for the first time while someone else is learning how to live without the person they thought they'd die beside. Both moments are equally sacred. Both are happening right now, while I sit here with cold coffee and warm thoughts.

Life is absurdly, heartbreakingly, wonderfully pointless. And that's exactly why it means everything. 

(Word count: 248)
